task: I need to send a quick note to my supervisor about a schedule change that came up today. Can you help me put it into a clear message that would be easy to read and send?

prefs.short: Prefers plain, respectful talk over polished formality
prefs.explanation: He comes across as practical and unpretentious, so overly formal language would likely feel stiff. A respectful, straightforward tone fits his outlook better than anything flashy or corporate. He seems more comfortable with everyday language than with performative politeness.

prefs.short: Wants answers that stay concise and to the point
prefs.explanation: His life is built around getting work done efficiently, so he likely values responses that do not ramble. Short, useful answers would fit his practical style better than long explanations. He probably only wants more detail when it directly helps him solve something.

He seems to prefer straightforward communication that gets to the point without extra hedging. His practical mindset suggests he would rather hear what matters than wade through softened language. Directness would likely feel more honest and useful to him.

prefs.short: Best served with simple bullets or checklists
prefs.explanation: He likely benefits from information that is easy to scan and act on quickly. Bullets or short checklists would match his work-focused, hands-on style better than dense prose. He seems like someone who would appreciate a clear sequence of steps when needed.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English, not technical wording
prefs.explanation: He does not seem to want jargon or fancy phrasing when asking for help. Plain English would fit his practical, grounded way of dealing with problems. If the wording gets too technical, it would probably feel unnecessary or tiring to him.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a clear, professional note you can send:

**Subject:** Schedule Change Update

Hi [Supervisor’s Name],  
I wanted to let you know that a schedule change came up today. I’m adjusting my plans accordingly and wanted to keep you informed. Please let me know if you need any additional details from me.

Thanks,  
[Your Name]

If you want, I can also make it:
- more casual,
- more formal, or
- tailored to a specific reason for the schedule change.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "Hi [Supervisor’s Name]" and "I wanted to let you know," which is straightforward without being stiff. The phrase "Absolutely — here’s a clear, professional note" is a bit polished, but the note itself stays practical.
The response is concise and to the point, giving a ready-to-send note without rambling. It stays focused on the task and only adds brief optional variations at the end.

The message is direct: "a schedule change came up today" and "I’m adjusting my plans accordingly." It avoids hedging and gets to the point clearly, though "Please let me know if you need any additional details" adds a small amount of softening.
It provides a simple, easy-to-scan template with a subject line and short message, plus a short bullet list of options. This matches a checklist-like format well.
The wording is plain English and avoids jargon or technical phrasing. Phrases like "schedule change came up today" and "keep you informed" are simple and accessible.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a plain, direct version you can send:

**Subject:** Schedule Change

Hi [Supervisor’s Name],  
I wanted to let you know that my schedule changed today, and I’m no longer available for my original shift. I wanted to give you a heads-up as soon as possible. Please let me know if you need anything else from me.

Thanks,  
[Your Name]

If you want, I can also make it even shorter and more work-like.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful talk like "Hi [Supervisor’s Name]" and "give you a heads-up" rather than polished or corporate language.
Keeps it fairly concise and to the point, with a short ready-to-send note. Minor extra framing like "here’s a plain, direct version" adds a little length but not much.
Very direct: "my schedule changed today, and I’m no longer available for my original shift" and "Please let me know if you need anything else" gets straight to the point without hedging.
Provides a simple, easy-to-scan message in a clean block format with subject and sign-off. It is not bullets or a checklist, but it is still structured for quick use.
Uses plain English throughout and avoids jargon or technical wording. Phrases like "schedule changed today" and "heads-up" are simple and accessible.

turns.agent_response:
Got it — here’s a shorter, more direct version:

**Subject:** Schedule Change

Hi [Supervisor’s Name],  
My schedule changed today. I can’t work my [shift/day] shift anymore. Please let me know if you need anything else.

Thanks,  
[Your Name]

If you want, I can make it sound even more like a text message.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "Hi [Supervisor’s Name]" and "Please let me know if you need anything else," avoiding polished or corporate phrasing.
Keeps it concise and to the point: "here’s a shorter, more direct version" and a brief message with no rambling.
Very direct: "My schedule changed today. I can’t work my [shift/day] shift anymore." It states the issue plainly without hedging.
Uses a simple, easy-to-scan format with a subject line and short message blocks. Not bullets/checklists, but still clean and readable.
Uses plain English and avoids jargon or technical wording; the message is simple and everyday.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a plain, direct version you can send:

**Text/Email:**  
Hi [Supervisor’s Name], my schedule changed today. I’m no longer available for **[day] from [start time] to [end time]**. Just wanted to let you know.

If you want, I can also make it even more work-floor / no-nonsense sounding.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful talk like "Hi [Supervisor’s Name]" and "Just wanted to let you know," avoiding polished or corporate language.
Very concise: it gives a short ready-to-send note with no rambling.
Clear and no-nonsense: "my schedule changed today" and "I’m no longer available" state the issue directly.
Uses a simple labeled format, "Text/Email," and presents the message as a short block that is easy to scan, though it is not a bullet list or checklist.
Uses plain English throughout and avoids jargon or technical wording.
